user.training_background: Experienced in both weight training and running; regularly runs 10 km and half marathon distances.
user.primary_goal: Hypertrophy
user.schedule_constraint: Can train up to 6 days per week, up to 90 minutes per session.
user.preferences: Wants to keep both lifting and running, enjoys structured training, and does not want to give up lower-body training.
user.dislikes: Does not want a plan that sacrifices either lifting or running completely.

solution_raw_text:
[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is hypertrophy, so lifting volume and quality are the main priority.
- Running is kept at two maintenance-focused sessions per week instead of a performance-building block.
- The plan uses four lifting days and two running days inside the six-day cap and 90-minute session limit.
- Lower-body lifting is preserved because the user explicitly does not want to give it up.
- Running sessions are separated from hard lower-body sessions to reduce interference and leg-fatigue spillover.

[2] 4-Week Training Plan
Week 1
- Week: 1
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Bench press, incline dumbbell press, chest-supported row, lat pulldown, lateral raise, and arm accessories; mostly 3 working sets per exercise.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8, leave 1 to 3 reps in reserve.
- Short reason: Opens the week with a high-quality hypertrophy session while the user is fresh.
- Week: 1
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 35 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides, then 10 minutes of calf, hip, and ankle mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 45
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2 effort.
- Short reason: Keeps running economy without creating heavy leg fatigue before the main lower day.
- Week: 1
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Back squat or hack squat, Romanian deadlift, leg press, leg curl, calf raise, and trunk work; 3 working sets on major lifts.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8, controlled tempo.
- Short reason: Preserves lower-body muscle stimulus as part of the primary goal.
- Week: 1
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Pull-up or pulldown, flat dumbbell press, cable row, machine shoulder press, rear-delt work, and arms; moderate volume.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 75
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Adds a second upper session for weekly hypertrophy volume without stressing the legs.
- Week: 1
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 40 minutes steady but sub-threshold running, followed by 8 minutes easy mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Mostly Zone 2 to low Zone 3, never race-like.
- Short reason: Maintains running feel while still leaving room for Saturday lifting.
- Week: 1
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Front squat or leg press, hip thrust, split squat, seated leg curl, calf raise, and optional adductor work; moderate volume.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7, do not grind reps.
- Short reason: A second lower day keeps weekly hypertrophy volume high without turning into a power-focused day.
- Week: 1
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Full rest or an easy 20-minute walk and light mobility only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Creates a clear recovery point before repeating the training week.

Week 2
- Week: 2
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Repeat Week 1 exercise selection; add a fourth set to bench press and chest-supported row if recovery is good.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Increases upper-body volume gradually to support hypertrophy.
- Week: 2
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 38 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides, then mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 48
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2.
- Short reason: Adds only a small running progression so lifting remains dominant.
- Week: 2
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Repeat Week 1 lower pattern; add one set to the first squat pattern or leg press only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 88
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Progresses lower-body volume modestly without overshooting recovery.
- Week: 2
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same template as Week 1 with one extra set on the primary pull or press movement.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 78
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Builds weekly hypertrophy volume while fatigue is still manageable.
- Week: 2
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 42 minutes steady running with the last 8 minutes slightly faster but still controlled.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 52
- Intensity / effort note: Zone 2 to low Zone 3.
- Short reason: Keeps running performance from drifting while staying secondary.
- Week: 2
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same movement pattern as Week 1, but add reps before adding load on split squats and curls.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 82
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7.
- Short reason: Extends lower-body work without colliding with the running goal.
- Week: 2
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Rest day with optional walk and soft-tissue work.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Protects recovery heading into Week 3.

Week 3
- Week: 3
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep Week 2 volume and try to progress load or reps slightly on the first two compound lifts.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on top sets, still controlled.
- Short reason: Week 3 is the highest-quality overload week for the primary goal.
- Week: 3
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 40 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2.
- Short reason: Maintains run frequency without turning Tuesday into a hard session.
- Week: 3
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercise list and aim for small rep or load progressions on squat and Romanian deadlift.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 88
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on main lifts, no failed reps.
- Short reason: Provides the strongest lower-body hypertrophy stimulus of the block.
- Week: 3
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same template as prior weeks; push one accessory movement slightly harder if recovery is solid.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Supports total hypertrophy volume without creating excessive systemic fatigue.
- Week: 3
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 40 minutes steady running, fully sub-threshold and conversational for most of the session.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Upper Zone 2 only.
- Short reason: Keeps running maintenance in place while protecting Saturday leg work.
- Week: 3
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Use the same movement pattern with controlled volume; avoid adding extra sets beyond the plan.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Maintains frequency while preventing interference from accumulated fatigue.
- Week: 3
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Full rest or an easy walk.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Sets up a smoother transition into the deload week.

Week 4
- Week: 4
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercises but remove roughly one set from each main movement and stay crisp technically.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 70
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Deloads fatigue while preserving movement quality and muscle stimulus.
- Week: 4
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 30 to 32 minutes easy running with no strides.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 35
- Intensity / effort note: Easy Zone 2.
- Short reason: Reduces total stress while keeping the running routine intact.
- Week: 4
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Same lower template with roughly 25 to 30 percent less volume than Week 3.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 72
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Allows leg recovery so the block ends feeling fresh instead of depleted.
- Week: 4
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Shortened upper session with moderate loads and fewer accessories.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 65
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Retains practice and blood flow without chasing overload.
- Week: 4
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Easy maintenance run
- Session details: 32 to 35 minutes easy running only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 35
- Intensity / effort note: Easy Zone 2.
- Short reason: Keeps the secondary goal present while clearly prioritizing recovery.
- Week: 4
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Short lower session with reduced sets on all lifts and no grinders.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 70
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Finishes the block with enough stimulus but much less fatigue.
- Week: 4
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Full rest.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 0
- Intensity / effort note: Rest.
- Short reason: Ends the four-week block with a clear recovery day.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan gives hypertrophy the first claim on recovery by using four lifting sessions and only two running sessions. Running is kept mostly easy or steady, not interval-heavy, so it supports maintenance without undermining lower-body growth. Lower-body training stays in the program twice per week because removing it would conflict with the user's preference and the primary physique goal.

[4] Safety Notes
- Do not turn the Friday run into a hard tempo or race-pace effort, especially before Saturday lower-body lifting.
- Keep at least one to three reps in reserve on most hypertrophy work to avoid unnecessary fatigue accumulation.
- If leg soreness remains unusually high after Wednesday, keep Saturday lower work moderate rather than adding volume.
- Running shoe choice and easy surfaces can help reduce lower-leg stress, but the plan still assumes normal healthy tolerance rather than medical management.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes the rhythm and baseline volume. Week 2 adds a small amount of lifting volume and a small amount of running time. Week 3 is the highest overload week, but running intensity is intentionally capped so the primary goal still wins. Week 4 reduces volume across both modalities so the user keeps momentum while shedding fatigue.

- Prioritize lifting quality and weekly hypertrophy volume over running progression.

original_plan:
[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is hypertrophy, so lifting volume and quality are the main priority.
- Running is kept at two maintenance-focused sessions per week instead of a performance-building block.
- The plan uses four lifting days and two running days inside the six-day cap and 90-minute session limit.
- Lower-body lifting is preserved because the user explicitly does not want to give it up.
- Running sessions are separated from hard lower-body sessions to reduce interference and leg-fatigue spillover.

[2] 4-Week Training Plan
Week 1
- Week: 1
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Bench press, incline dumbbell press, chest-supported row, lat pulldown, lateral raise, and arm accessories; mostly 3 working sets per exercise.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8, leave 1 to 3 reps in reserve.
- Short reason: Opens the week with a high-quality hypertrophy session while the user is fresh.
- Week: 1
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 35 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides, then 10 minutes of calf, hip, and ankle mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 45
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2 effort.
- Short reason: Keeps running economy without creating heavy leg fatigue before the main lower day.
- Week: 1
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Back squat or hack squat, Romanian deadlift, leg press, leg curl, calf raise, and trunk work; 3 working sets on major lifts.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8, controlled tempo.
- Short reason: Preserves lower-body muscle stimulus as part of the primary goal.
- Week: 1
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Pull-up or pulldown, flat dumbbell press, cable row, machine shoulder press, rear-delt work, and arms; moderate volume.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 75
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Adds a second upper session for weekly hypertrophy volume without stressing the legs.
- Week: 1
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 40 minutes steady but sub-threshold running, followed by 8 minutes easy mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Mostly Zone 2 to low Zone 3, never race-like.
- Short reason: Maintains running feel while still leaving room for Saturday lifting.
- Week: 1
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Front squat or leg press, hip thrust, split squat, seated leg curl, calf raise, and optional adductor work; moderate volume.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7, do not grind reps.
- Short reason: A second lower day keeps weekly hypertrophy volume high without turning into a power-focused day.
- Week: 1
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Full rest or an easy 20-minute walk and light mobility only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Creates a clear recovery point before repeating the training week.

Week 2
- Week: 2
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Repeat Week 1 exercise selection; add a fourth set to bench press and chest-supported row if recovery is good.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Increases upper-body volume gradually to support hypertrophy.
- Week: 2
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 38 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides, then mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 48
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2.
- Short reason: Adds only a small running progression so lifting remains dominant.
- Week: 2
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Repeat Week 1 lower pattern; add one set to the first squat pattern or leg press only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 88
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Progresses lower-body volume modestly without overshooting recovery.
- Week: 2
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same template as Week 1 with one extra set on the primary pull or press movement.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 78
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Builds weekly hypertrophy volume while fatigue is still manageable.
- Week: 2
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 42 minutes steady running with the last 8 minutes slightly faster but still controlled.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 52
- Intensity / effort note: Zone 2 to low Zone 3.
- Short reason: Keeps running performance from drifting while staying secondary.
- Week: 2
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same movement pattern as Week 1, but add reps before adding load on split squats and curls.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 82
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7.
- Short reason: Extends lower-body work without colliding with the running goal.
- Week: 2
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Rest day with optional walk and soft-tissue work.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Protects recovery heading into Week 3.

Week 3
- Week: 3
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep Week 2 volume and try to progress load or reps slightly on the first two compound lifts.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on top sets, still controlled.
- Short reason: Week 3 is the highest-quality overload week for the primary goal.
- Week: 3
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run
- Session details: 40 minutes easy running plus 4 x 20-second relaxed strides.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2.
- Short reason: Maintains run frequency without turning Tuesday into a hard session.
- Week: 3
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercise list and aim for small rep or load progressions on squat and Romanian deadlift.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 88
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on main lifts, no failed reps.

- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B
- Session details: Same template as prior weeks; push one accessory movement slightly harder if recovery is solid.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Supports total hypertrophy volume without creating excessive systemic fatigue.
- Week: 3

- Main focus: Steady maintenance run
- Session details: 40 minutes steady running, fully sub-threshold and conversational for most of the session.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Upper Zone 2 only.
- Short reason: Keeps running maintenance in place while protecting Saturday leg work.
- Week: 3

- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B

- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Maintains frequency while preventing interference from accumulated fatigue.

- Main focus: Rest and recovery

Week 4
- Week: 4
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercises but remove roughly one set from each main movement and stay crisp technically.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 70
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.

- Week: 4

- Session details: 30 to 32 minutes easy running with no strides.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 35
- Intensity / effort note: Easy Zone 2.
- Short reason: Reduces total stress while keeping the running routine intact.
- Week: 4
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Same lower template with roughly 25 to 30 percent less volume than Week 3.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 72
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.

- Estimated duration (minutes): 35

- Day: Saturday

- Estimated duration (minutes): 70
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Finishes the block with enough stimulus but much less fatigue.
- Week: 4
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest and recovery
- Session details: Full rest.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 0
- Intensity / effort note: Rest.
- Short reason: Ends the four-week block with a clear recovery day.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan gives hypertrophy the first claim on recovery by using four lifting sessions and only two running sessions. Running is kept mostly easy or steady, not interval-heavy, so it supports maintenance without undermining lower-body growth. Lower-body training stays in the program twice per week because removing it would conflict with the user's preference and the primary physique goal.

[4] Safety Notes
- Do not turn the Friday run into a hard tempo or race-pace effort, especially before Saturday lower-body lifting.
- Keep at least one to three reps in reserve on most hypertrophy work to avoid unnecessary fatigue accumulation.
- If leg soreness remains unusually high after Wednesday, keep Saturday lower work moderate rather than adding volume.
- Running shoe choice and easy surfaces can help reduce lower-leg stress, but the plan still assumes normal healthy tolerance rather than medical management.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes the rhythm and baseline volume. Week 2 adds a small amount of lifting volume and a small amount of running time. Week 3 is the highest overload week, but running intensity is intentionally capped so the primary goal still wins. Week 4 reduces volume across both modalities so the user keeps momentum while shedding fatigue.

user.training_background: Experienced in both weight training and running; regularly runs 10 km and half marathon distances.
user.primary_goal: Hypertrophy
user.schedule_constraint: Can train up to 6 days per week, up to 90 minutes per session.
user.preferences: Wants to keep both lifting and running, enjoys structured training, and does not want to give up lower-body training.
user.dislikes: Does not want a plan that sacrifices either lifting or running completely.

Week 3

- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A

- Estimated duration (minutes): 85
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on top sets, still controlled.
- Short reason: Week 3 is the highest-quality overload week for the primary goal.

- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Easy aerobic run

- Estimated duration (minutes): 50
- Intensity / effort note: Comfortable Zone 2.
- Short reason: Maintains run frequency without turning Tuesday into a hard session.
- Week: 3
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercise list and aim for small rep or load progressions on squat and Romanian deadlift.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 88
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 8 on main lifts, no failed reps.
- Short reason: Provides the strongest lower-body hypertrophy stimulus of the block.
- Week: 3
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy B

- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.

- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B

Week 4
- Week: 4
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy A
- Session details: Keep the same exercises but remove roughly one set from each main movement and stay crisp technically.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 70

- Day: Tuesday

- Estimated duration (minutes): 35

- Week: 4

- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy A

- Week: 4
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy B

- Intensity / effort note: Rest.
- Short reason: Ends the four-week block with a clear recovery day.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan gives hypertrophy the first claim on recovery by using four lifting sessions and only two running sessions. Running is kept mostly easy or steady, not interval-heavy, so it supports maintenance without undermining lower-body growth. Lower-body training stays in the program twice per week because removing it would conflict with the user's preference and the primary physique goal.

[4] Safety Notes
- Do not turn the Friday run into a hard tempo or race-pace effort, especially before Saturday lower-body lifting.
- Keep at least one to three reps in reserve on most hypertrophy work to avoid unnecessary fatigue accumulation.
- If leg soreness remains unusually high after Wednesday, keep Saturday lower work moderate rather than adding volume.
- Running shoe choice and easy surfaces can help reduce lower-leg stress, but the plan still assumes normal healthy tolerance rather than medical management.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes the rhythm and baseline volume. Week 2 adds a small amount of lifting volume and a small amount of running time. Week 3 is the highest overload week, but running intensity is intentionally capped so the primary goal still wins. Week 4 reduces volume across both modalities so the user keeps momentum while shedding fatigue.

- Primary goal is beginner-friendly strength and muscle gain, so the plan stays gym-centered.
- Running is minimized because the user prefers gym training and does not enjoy running-focused plans.

- Progression is simple and repeatable so recovery and technique can improve together.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps nearly all training stress in gym-based strength and hypertrophy work because that matches the user's preference and beginner needs. Conditioning is optional and easy so it improves general fitness without competing with recovery, flexibility work, or skill acquisition in the gym.

[4] Safety Notes
- Warm up the hips, ankles, shoulders, and thoracic spine before every gym session because low flexibility is a meaningful limiter.
- Use stable exercise variations and comfortable ranges of motion instead of forcing deep positions.
- Avoid high-skill barbell lifts until the user has a more consistent technical base.
- Do not add running-focused work just to fill time, because it does not support the primary goal or preference set.

Week 1 teaches the pattern. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume. Week 3 uses the same structure with slightly stronger loading if technique remains stable. Week 4 trims volume to keep the beginner adapting rather than burning out.

original_plan:
[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is beginner-friendly strength and muscle gain, so the plan stays gym-centered.
- Running is minimized because the user prefers gym training and does not enjoy running-focused plans.
- Low flexibility is handled by adding warm-up and mobility time instead of pushing high-skill lifts too early.
- The schedule fits three main training days with one optional easy conditioning day inside the 3 to 4 day limit.
- Progression is simple and repeatable so recovery and technique can improve together.

[2] 4-Week Training Plan
Week 1
- Week: 1
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Full-body gym A
- Session details: Leg press, dumbbell bench press, chest-supported row, Romanian deadlift with light dumbbells, cable pulldown, and plank work; 2 to 3 working sets each after 8 minutes of mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 60
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Introduces basic movement patterns without overwhelming a beginner.
- Week: 1
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Rest
- Session details: Full rest or an easy 15-minute walk.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 15
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Creates recovery space after the first gym day.
- Week: 1
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Full-body gym B
- Session details: Goblet squat to a comfortable depth, machine chest press, seated row, split squat holding support, hamstring curl, and dead-bug core work.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 60
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Builds general strength with stable, accessible exercises.
- Week: 1
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Rest
- Session details: Rest and light stretching for hips, ankles, and thoracic spine.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 15
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Keeps flexibility work in the routine without adding fatigue.
- Week: 1
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Full-body gym A
- Session details: Repeat Monday with slightly more confidence in movement execution but no need to push heavy.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 60
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6 to 7.
- Short reason: Repetition helps a beginner learn technique faster.
- Week: 1
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Optional easy conditioning
- Session details: 20 minutes incline treadmill walk or easy bike plus 10 minutes mobility.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 30
- Intensity / effort note: Easy conversational effort.
- Short reason: Improves general fitness without turning the plan into a running program.

- Estimated duration (minutes): 50

- Session details: 15 to 20 minutes easy bike or treadmill walk.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Easy.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps nearly all training stress in gym-based strength and hypertrophy work because that matches the user's preference and beginner needs. Conditioning is optional and easy so it improves general fitness without competing with recovery, flexibility work, or skill acquisition in the gym.

[4] Safety Notes
- Warm up the hips, ankles, shoulders, and thoracic spine before every gym session because low flexibility is a meaningful limiter.
- Use stable exercise variations and comfortable ranges of motion instead of forcing deep positions.
- Avoid high-skill barbell lifts until the user has a more consistent technical base.
- Do not add running-focused work just to fill time, because it does not support the primary goal or preference set.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 teaches the pattern. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume. Week 3 uses the same structure with slightly stronger loading if technique remains stable. Week 4 trims volume to keep the beginner adapting rather than burning out.

- Primary goal is fat loss while maintaining muscle, so resistance training remains the anchor of the week.
- Running is treated as conditioning support rather than a performance goal, which matches the user's preference.
- Frequent calf issues mean impact volume must stay conservative and replaceable with low-impact options.
- The weekly structure uses four lifting sessions and one conditioning-focused day inside the five-day limit.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
Because the primary goal is fat loss with muscle maintenance, lifting volume stays high enough to preserve muscle while conditioning stays supportive rather than competitive. Running appears only in conservative, flexible doses, and low-impact conditioning carries more of the aerobic workload so the calf and recovery budget are protected.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep running easy and replace it with incline walking, bike, or elliptical if calf discomfort rises.
- Do not stack extra intervals or long runs on top of the plan because that would push conditioning beyond its intended role.
- Maintain resistance training quality and avoid turning lower-body days into exhaustion sessions.
- If recovery drops, reduce conditioning first rather than cutting the muscle-preserving lifting work.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes the split and conservative conditioning volume. Week 2 adds a small amount of training work. Week 3 is the highest resistance-training week, but running still stays modest. Week 4 reduces both lifting and conditioning volume so the user can recover without losing rhythm.

original_plan:
[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is fat loss while maintaining muscle, so resistance training remains the anchor of the week.
- Running is treated as conditioning support rather than a performance goal, which matches the user's preference.
- Frequent calf issues mean impact volume must stay conservative and replaceable with low-impact options.
- The weekly structure uses four lifting sessions and one conditioning-focused day inside the five-day limit.
- The plan avoids high aerobic volume that could undermine muscle retention or aggravate the calf.

[2] 4-Week Training Plan
Week 1
- Week: 1
- Day: Monday
- Main focus: Upper body strength-hypertrophy
- Session details: Bench press or machine press, row, pulldown, incline dumbbell press, lateral raise, and arms; 3 working sets each.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 75
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Keeps upper-body muscle stimulus high for maintenance during fat loss.
- Week: 1
- Day: Tuesday
- Main focus: Lower body strength-hypertrophy
- Session details: Leg press or squat variation, Romanian deadlift, split squat, hamstring curl, calf raise with conservative volume, and trunk work.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7.
- Short reason: Supports lower-body muscle retention without turning the day into a running conflict.
- Week: 1
- Day: Wednesday
- Main focus: Low-impact conditioning
- Session details: 25 minutes incline walk, bike, or elliptical plus 15 minutes mobility and core work.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 45
- Intensity / effort note: Easy to moderate, mostly conversational.
- Short reason: Adds caloric expenditure with lower calf risk than performance running.
- Week: 1
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Upper hypertrophy
- Session details: Machine press, cable row, pulldown, chest fly, rear-delt work, and arms; moderate volume.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 70
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7 to 8.
- Short reason: Maintains weekly upper-body volume for muscle retention.
- Week: 1
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Rest
- Session details: Full rest or easy walk only.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Very easy.
- Short reason: Protects recovery before the second lower session.
- Week: 1
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Lower hypertrophy and conditioning finisher
- Session details: Leg press, hip hinge, glute bridge, leg curl, and optional 8 to 10 minutes easy sled push or bike if the calf feels calm.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 80
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 7, no grinding.
- Short reason: Keeps the lower-body stimulus strong while using conditioning as a small add-on.
- Week: 1
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Easy run or run-walk
- Session details: 20 to 25 minutes easy run-walk if the calf feels good; otherwise replace with incline walk.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 30
- Intensity / effort note: Easy conversational effort only.
- Short reason: Preserves a bit of running routine without making it a performance block.

Week 2

- Session details: Repeat Week 1 and add a set to the first press or row if recovery is good.

- Short reason: Slightly progresses muscle-maintenance volume.

Week 4
- Week: 4

- Day: Sunday

- Estimated duration (minutes): 20
- Intensity / effort note: Easy.
- Short reason: Makes recovery the priority while preserving routine continuity.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
Because the primary goal is fat loss with muscle maintenance, lifting volume stays high enough to preserve muscle while conditioning stays supportive rather than competitive. Running appears only in conservative, flexible doses, and low-impact conditioning carries more of the aerobic workload so the calf and recovery budget are protected.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep running easy and replace it with incline walking, bike, or elliptical if calf discomfort rises.
- Do not stack extra intervals or long runs on top of the plan because that would push conditioning beyond its intended role.
- Maintain resistance training quality and avoid turning lower-body days into exhaustion sessions.
- If recovery drops, reduce conditioning first rather than cutting the muscle-preserving lifting work.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes the split and conservative conditioning volume. Week 2 adds a small amount of training work. Week 3 is the highest resistance-training week, but running still stays modest. Week 4 reduces both lifting and conditioning volume so the user can recover without losing rhythm.

Because the primary goal is fat loss with muscle maintenance, lifting volume stays high enough to preserve muscle while conditioning stays supportive rather than competitive. Running appears only in conservative, flexible doses, and low-impact conditioning carries more of the aerobic workload so the calf and recovery budget are protected.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep running easy and replace it with incline walking, bike, or elliptical if calf discomfort rises.

- If recovery drops, reduce conditioning first rather than cutting the muscle-preserving lifting work.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan favors a simple gym habit over trying to improve everything at once. Running appears only as an optional easy add-on because the user mainly wants a sustainable gym routine, and any extra complexity would work against the primary goal of consistency.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep every session simple and stop well before failure because the user is new to structured training.
- Do not add extra workout variety just for novelty, since complexity is a risk factor for dropout here.
- Optional jogging should stay light and should be skipped if it makes the week feel harder to sustain.
- If soreness builds, keep the same schedule but reduce sets rather than adding catch-up sessions.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes a repeatable three-day routine. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume. Week 3 uses slightly stronger loading while keeping the same simple structure. Week 4 reduces volume so the user ends the month feeling capable of continuing.

Running is clearly the main objective, so the week is organized around two quality run slots and one easy run. Lifting appears twice as efficient full-body support, with controlled lower-body volume so it improves aesthetics and durability without blunting running performance or enjoyment.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep lower-body lifting submaximal and avoid turning support sessions into heavy leg-destruction days.
- If the legs feel unusually flat before a key run, reduce Tuesday or Saturday lower-body volume first.
- Do not expand lifting into a high-volume bodybuilding split because it would conflict with the stated priority.
- Running quality should feel controlled, not like repeated race efforts every week.

Week 1 sets the run-lift rhythm. Week 2 modestly increases run quality and easy mileage. Week 3 reaches the highest run-specific demand while keeping lifting stable. Week 4 trims volume across both modalities so recovery and enjoyment remain high.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
Running is clearly the main objective, so the week is organized around two quality run slots and one easy run. Lifting appears twice as efficient full-body support, with controlled lower-body volume so it improves aesthetics and durability without blunting running performance or enjoyment.

[4] Safety Notes
- Keep lower-body lifting submaximal and avoid turning support sessions into heavy leg-destruction days.
- If the legs feel unusually flat before a key run, reduce Tuesday or Saturday lower-body volume first.
- Do not expand lifting into a high-volume bodybuilding split because it would conflict with the stated priority.
- Running quality should feel controlled, not like repeated race efforts every week.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 sets the run-lift rhythm. Week 2 modestly increases run quality and easy mileage. Week 3 reaches the highest run-specific demand while keeping lifting stable. Week 4 trims volume across both modalities so recovery and enjoyment remain high.

user.dislikes: Does not want a bodybuilding-style plan to dominate the schedule.

Week 1 sets the run-lift rhythm. Week 2 modestly increases run quality and easy mileage. Week 3 reaches the highest run-specific demand while keeping lifting stable. Week 4 trims volume across both modalities so recovery and enjoyment remain high.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan balances running and lifting evenly because the main goal is sustainable hybrid training, but upper-body exercise safety outranks generic performance programming. Running can stay fairly normal, while gym work is individualized around machines, unilateral patterns, modified ranges, and grip-friendly options so the program remains realistic and safe.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid fixed bilateral barbell presses or any overhead position that feels unstable with the left upper limb.
- Use machine, cable, dumbbell, unilateral, or grip-assisted variations when they improve control and comfort.
- Do not force symmetrical loading or identical range of motion side to side if the limitation makes that unrealistic.
- Running is fine to maintain, but upper-body exercise selection should always stay subordinate to safe execution.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes safe movement choices. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume or duration. Week 3 is the modest high point, but upper-body choices remain conservative and individualized. Week 4 reduces volume so the plan feels sustainable enough to continue long term.

- Week: 4

- Week: 4

- Main focus: Upper body modified strength-hypertrophy
- Session details: Shorter upper-body session using the same safe machine, cable, and unilateral movements.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 60
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6.
- Short reason: Maintains safe upper-body practice while unloading fatigue.
- Week: 4
- Day: Thursday
- Main focus: Rest
- Session details: Full rest.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 0
- Intensity / effort note: Rest.
- Short reason: Protects recovery.
- Week: 4
- Day: Friday
- Main focus: Full-body hybrid gym session
- Session details: Shortened full-body session with reduced sets and no forced heavy bilateral pressing.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 65
- Intensity / effort note: RPE 6.
- Short reason: Ends the block in a sustainable place.
- Week: 4
- Day: Saturday
- Main focus: Steady run
- Session details: 35 minutes easy steady running.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 35
- Intensity / effort note: Zone 2.
- Short reason: Keeps running present without adding wear.
- Week: 4
- Day: Sunday
- Main focus: Rest
- Session details: Full rest.
- Estimated duration (minutes): 0
- Intensity / effort note: Rest.
- Short reason: Closes the block with recovery.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan balances running and lifting evenly because the main goal is sustainable hybrid training, but upper-body exercise safety outranks generic performance programming. Running can stay fairly normal, while gym work is individualized around machines, unilateral patterns, modified ranges, and grip-friendly options so the program remains realistic and safe.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid fixed bilateral barbell presses or any overhead position that feels unstable with the left upper limb.
- Use machine, cable, dumbbell, unilateral, or grip-assisted variations when they improve control and comfort.
- Do not force symmetrical loading or identical range of motion side to side if the limitation makes that unrealistic.
- Running is fine to maintain, but upper-body exercise selection should always stay subordinate to safe execution.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes safe movement choices. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume or duration. Week 3 is the modest high point, but upper-body choices remain conservative and individualized. Week 4 reduces volume so the plan feels sustainable enough to continue long term.

user.training_background: Consistently active; has completed a half marathon and also does regular weight training.
user.primary_goal: Sustainable long-term health and balanced hybrid training
user.secondary_goal: Maintain both running ability and general strength
user.schedule_constraint: 4 to 5 days per week, 60 to 80 minutes per session.
user.injury_or_limitation: Has a congenital limitation in the left upper limb, with reduced shoulder range of motion and weaker grip strength on that side.
user.preferences: Wants a balanced and realistic plan and wants to keep both running and gym training.
user.dislikes: Does not want a generic one-size-fits-all plan that ignores his physical limitation.

Week 1 establishes safe movement choices. Week 2 adds a small amount of volume or duration. Week 3 is the modest high point, but upper-body choices remain conservative and individualized. Week 4 reduces volume so the plan feels sustainable enough to continue long term.

The plan intentionally minimizes conditioning and focuses on supported strength work because the user's primary need is basic muscle and strength gain, not calorie burn or running fitness. That trade-off keeps the program less intimidating and better aligned with frequent lower-back discomfort and beginner recovery capacity.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid heavy axial loading or aggressive barbell work early in the process.
- Use supported machines, bodyweight boxes, and dumbbells to reduce lower-back stress.
- Do not add running sessions just to increase activity, because that would raise fatigue without serving the primary goal.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan intentionally minimizes conditioning and focuses on supported strength work because the user's primary need is basic muscle and strength gain, not calorie burn or running fitness. That trade-off keeps the program less intimidating and better aligned with frequent lower-back discomfort and beginner recovery capacity.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid heavy axial loading or aggressive barbell work early in the process.
- Use supported machines, bodyweight boxes, and dumbbells to reduce lower-back stress.
- Do not add running sessions just to increase activity, because that would raise fatigue without serving the primary goal.
- If any movement clearly increases back discomfort, shorten the range or substitute a more supported pattern.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 introduces safe patterns. Week 2 adds a very small amount of work if tolerated. Week 3 is the highest week but still conservative. Week 4 lowers volume so the user finishes feeling capable of continuing the habit.

[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is safe, consistent exercise without worsening knee pain.
- Lower-body work stays in the plan, but exercise choices and cardio modes are knee-friendly.
- The schedule uses three main training days plus one optional low-impact conditioning day inside the 3 to 4 day limit.
- The plan avoids impact-heavy running, jumping, and deep repetitive knee flexion.
- Progression is cautious and focuses on consistency, strength support, and symptom-aware exercise selection.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps lower-body training in place because removing it entirely would limit fitness and body-composition progress, but it shifts exercise choice toward posterior-chain work, controlled depth, and low-impact conditioning. That trade-off protects the knee while still allowing steady training progress.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid jumping, impact-heavy running, or deep repetitive knee flexion if those patterns aggravate symptoms.
- Use pain-tolerable ranges of motion and adjust depth before adding load.
- Low-impact cardio such as cycling or elliptical work is preferred over aggressive running.
- If the knee is more irritated after lower-body day, reduce lower-body range or volume before removing training altogether.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes safe movement choices. Week 2 adds only small progressions if the knee tolerated Week 1. Week 3 is the modest high point, mostly through upper-body and posterior-chain progress. Week 4 reduces volume so consistency remains high and symptoms are less likely to build.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps lower-body training in place because removing it entirely would limit fitness and body-composition progress, but it shifts exercise choice toward posterior-chain work, controlled depth, and low-impact conditioning. That trade-off protects the knee while still allowing steady training progress.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid jumping, impact-heavy running, or deep repetitive knee flexion if those patterns aggravate symptoms.
- Use pain-tolerable ranges of motion and adjust depth before adding load.
- Low-impact cardio such as cycling or elliptical work is preferred over aggressive running.
- If the knee is more irritated after lower-body day, reduce lower-body range or volume before removing training altogether.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes safe movement choices. Week 2 adds only small progressions if the knee tolerated Week 1. Week 3 is the modest high point, mostly through upper-body and posterior-chain progress. Week 4 reduces volume so consistency remains high and symptoms are less likely to build.

Step 1. Extract the user profile
- Primary goal: Rebuild a sustainable exercise routine
- Secondary goal: Recover fitness and physique
- Experience level: Likes both weight training and running; previously trained a lot, but is currently inconsistent.
- Schedule constraints: Wants to train 4 days per week, around 60 minutes per session, but adherence may be unstable.
- Injury / physical limitations: No major injury; physically healthy.
- Preferences: Likes both running and lifting.
- Likely trade-offs or conflicts: The main conflict is between liking both lifting and running and the reality that adherence is unstable right now, so volume must stay realistic.

Step 2. Planning strategy
- Prioritize adherence and realistic weekly rhythm over ambitious training stress.
- Keep both lifting and running in the plan because that supports motivation, but make both manageable.
- Build in rest days and an easier final week so the restart is sustainable.
- Use repeatable full-body training rather than complex splits or performance blocks.

- Primary goal is general fitness and a healthy habit, so the plan stays simple, indoor-friendly, and beginner-appropriate.
- Lower-back discomfort means exercise choice should limit heavy spinal loading and high-skill barbell work.
- The user prefers treadmill running, so cardio stays indoors and low-complexity.
- The schedule uses three core days and one optional lighter day inside the 3 to 4 day limit.
- Progression is gentle so the habit can grow without back flare-ups or intimidation.

The plan keeps both gym work and treadmill cardio in the week, but the gym sessions stay simple and back-aware while cardio stays easy and indoor-based. That trade-off supports the primary habit goal better than a more intense or outdoor-focused program would.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid heavy free-weight spinal loading and high-skill barbell movements while the lower back is a recurring issue.
- Use indoor treadmill work at easy effort rather than building an outdoor running program.
- Keep core work simple and supportive instead of aggressive fatigue-based circuits.
- If the back feels more irritated after gym day, reduce lower-body load or switch to more supported machine work.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes an indoor-friendly routine. Week 2 adds a small amount of work. Week 3 is the strongest week, still within beginner limits. Week 4 reduces volume so the user can continue the habit comfortably.

- Primary goal is general fitness and a healthy habit, so the plan stays simple, indoor-friendly, and beginner-appropriate.
- Lower-back discomfort means exercise choice should limit heavy spinal loading and high-skill barbell work.
- The user prefers treadmill running, so cardio stays indoors and low-complexity.
- The schedule uses three core days and one optional lighter day inside the 3 to 4 day limit.
- Progression is gentle so the habit can grow without back flare-ups or intimidation.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps both gym work and treadmill cardio in the week, but the gym sessions stay simple and back-aware while cardio stays easy and indoor-based. That trade-off supports the primary habit goal better than a more intense or outdoor-focused program would.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid heavy free-weight spinal loading and high-skill barbell movements while the lower back is a recurring issue.
- Use indoor treadmill work at easy effort rather than building an outdoor running program.
- Keep core work simple and supportive instead of aggressive fatigue-based circuits.
- If the back feels more irritated after gym day, reduce lower-body load or switch to more supported machine work.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes an indoor-friendly routine. Week 2 adds a small amount of work. Week 3 is the strongest week, still within beginner limits. Week 4 reduces volume so the user can continue the habit comfortably.

user.training_background: Beginner; likes gym workouts and treadmill running.
user.primary_goal: Improve general fitness and build a healthy exercise habit
user.schedule_constraint: 3 to 4 days per week, 50 to 60 minutes per session.
user.injury_or_limitation: Frequent lower-back pain due to carrying a heavy bag.
user.preferences: Prefers indoor exercise and treadmill running.
user.dislikes: Likely to dislike plans centered on outdoor running or high-skill barbell movements.

solution_raw_text:
[1] Goal and Constraint Summary
- Primary goal is general fitness and a healthy habit, so the plan stays simple, indoor-friendly, and beginner-appropriate.
- Lower-back discomfort means exercise choice should limit heavy spinal loading and high-skill barbell work.
- The user prefers treadmill running, so cardio stays indoors and low-complexity.
- The schedule uses three core days and one optional lighter day inside the 3 to 4 day limit.
- Progression is gentle so the habit can grow without back flare-ups or intimidation.

[3] Trade-off Explanation
The plan keeps both gym work and treadmill cardio in the week, but the gym sessions stay simple and back-aware while cardio stays easy and indoor-based. That trade-off supports the primary habit goal better than a more intense or outdoor-focused program would.

[4] Safety Notes
- Avoid heavy free-weight spinal loading and high-skill barbell movements while the lower back is a recurring issue.
- Use indoor treadmill work at easy effort rather than building an outdoor running program.
- Keep core work simple and supportive instead of aggressive fatigue-based circuits.
- If the back feels more irritated after gym day, reduce lower-body load or switch to more supported machine work.

[5] Progression Logic
Week 1 establishes an indoor-friendly routine. Week 2 adds a small amount of work. Week 3 is the strongest week, still within beginner limits. Week 4 reduces volume so the user can continue the habit comfortably.
